content_en:
The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah (6 AH / 628 CE) stands as one of the most diplomatically sophisticated and theologically rich events of the Prophetic seerah. Described by the Quran as 'a clear victory' (fath mubin) despite appearing to contemporaries as a humiliating concession, it is a masterclass in prophetic statecraft and a demonstration of the divine perspective that operates on a plane invisible to immediate human perception.

In the sixth year of Hijra, the Prophet ﷺ set out with approximately 1,400 Companions — armed only with travel swords — to perform the lesser pilgrimage (Umrah) to Mecca. When the Quraysh learned of his approach, they dispatched forces to block his path. The Prophet's ﷺ camel sat down at Hudaybiyyah on the outskirts of the sacred territory — an act he interpreted as a divine instruction to halt — and negotiations began.

The resulting treaty, dictated by the Quraysh envoy Suhayl ibn Amr, contained terms that most Companions found deeply objectionable. The Muslims agreed to return to Medina without performing Umrah that year; to return to Mecca the following year for three days only; to return any Meccan who came to Medina as a Muslim without the permission of his guardian; while Medina would not be obligated to return Medinan Muslims who fled to Mecca. Furthermore, the treaty's preamble was altered at Qurayshi insistence — the phrase 'Muhammad, Messenger of Allah' was replaced with 'Muhammad ibn Abdullah,' as the Quraysh refused to acknowledge his prophetic status.

Umar ibn al-Khattab, who was present, reacted with barely contained outrage: 'Is he not the Messenger of Allah? Are we not Muslims? Why should we accept this humiliation in our religion?' Abu Bakr counseled patience, saying: 'Remain firm on what the Prophet ﷺ says. I bear witness that he is the Messenger of Allah.' The Prophet ﷺ himself asked Ali to erase the words 'Messenger of Allah' from the treaty document — an act Ali found so painful he could not do it until the Prophet ﷺ pointed to it and did it himself.

The Quran then descended: 'Indeed, We have given you a clear victory' (Quran 48:1). What appeared as defeat was, from the divine perspective, the greatest opening Islam had yet achieved. For the first time, the Quraysh had legally recognized the Islamic state as a legitimate political entity — an equal party to a formal treaty. The clause that Companions had found most objectionable — that Meccan Muslims who fled to Medina must be returned — in practice worked to Muslim advantage: those returned did not apostatize but instead preached Islam in Mecca and radicalized those around them. Within two years of Hudaybiyyah, the number of people entering Islam exceeded all previous totals combined, as the Quran had foretold: 'And you will see the people entering into the religion of Allah in multitudes' (Quran 110:2). The subsequent conquest of Mecca, just two years later, vindicated the Prophet's ﷺ vision completely.
